--- SOURCE 1: Microbial Electrosynthesis Conversion Pathway ---
URL: https://www.nrel.gov/bioenergy/co2-utilization-economics/microbial-electrosynthesis-conversion-pathway

SUMMARY:
Current – published results in the open literature with electricity priced at $0.068/kilowatt-hours (kWh) and a CO₂ cost of $40/metric ton (MT)

FULL CONTENT:
![NREL logo](/images/libraries/global/nrel.svg?sfvrsn=d16c1421_4)

# Microbial Electrosynthesis Conversion Pathway

Learn about the economics of producing methane, acetic acid, formic acid, and ethanol
from carbon dioxide (CO2) and electricity via microbial electrosynthesis.

![Container filled with microbes](/images/libraries/bioenergy-images/pathway-icon-mes.png?sfvrsn=128c5217_2 "pathway-icon-mes")

For each product developed using this conversion pathway, visualizations show key
cost and conversion metrics based on three different cases:

## Select a Product and Case

## Methane

As of 2018, the estimated global production of methane reached 250 million metric
tons per year with applications in energy and as a hydrogen precursor, selling for
an average $0.16/kg ($0.13–$0.23 between 2014 and 2018).

The biggest cost contributors for producing methane from carbon dioxide and electricity
via microbial electrosynthesis include capital costs and maintenance for the current
case and capital costs and electricity feedstock cost for the future and theoretical
cases.

## Acetic Acid

Acetic acid is a food additive and chemical precursor for vinyl acetate and terephthalic
acid. Global production is estimated at 19–20 million metric tons per year at $0.61/kg
($0.50–$0.71 between 2014 and 2018).

The biggest cost contributors for producing acetic acid from carbon dioxide and electricity
via microbial electrosynthesis include capital costs and electricity feedstock cost
for the current case and capital costs for the future and theoretical cases.

## Formic Acid

Formic acid is used in pharmaceuticals, food additives, and leather/tanning applications.
Global production of formic acid totals approximately 1 million metric tons per year
at $0.66/kg ($0.66–$0.67 between 2014 and 2016).

The biggest cost contributors for producing formic acid from carbon dioxide and electricity
via microbial electrosynthesis include capital costs and maintenance for the current
case and capital costs for the future and theoretical cases.

## Ethanol

Ethanol is used primarily as fuel blendstock and feedstock in synthetic chemical routes.
Global production recently reached approximately 77 million metric tons per year at
$0.51/kg ($0.42–$0.72 between 2014 and 2018).

The biggest cost contributors for producing ethanol from carbon dioxide and electricity
via microbial electrosynthesis include capital costs and maintenance for the current
case and capital costs for the future and theoretical cases.

URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1192187/fmicb-14-1192187-HTML-r1/image_m/fmicb-14-1192187-g001.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts a microbial electrosynthesis system with an ion exchange membrane separating an anode and cathode, showing electrons and protons involved in CO2 conversion into organic products like acids, bioplastics, and biofuels.

URL: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Deepak-Pant-3/publication/341071197/figure/fig4/AS:11431281153639447@1682519672952/Schematic-representation-of-microbial-electrosynthesis-system-toward-CO2-reduction-for.tif
DESCRIPTION: None

URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1192187/fmicb-14-1192187-HTML-r1/image_m/fmicb-14-1192187-gr0001.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A diagram illustrating current microbial electrosynthesis processes for converting CO2 into organic acids, bioplastics, and biofuels, including electrochemical reactions at biofilms, pathways for CO2 fixation, and electron transfer mechanisms within microbial electrolysis cells.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/759678/fenrg-09-759678-HTML/image_m/fenrg-09-759678-g004.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image contains three line graphs illustrating current density (i) versus potential (E/V vs. Ag/AgCl) at different feed rates, showing the effects of inoculation and biocathode addition on microbial electrosynthesis of CO2, with curves for before inoculation, after inoculation, and with biocathode.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1192187/fmicb-14-1192187-HTML-r1/image_m/fmicb-14-1192187-g005.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image shows three schematic diagrams illustrating different methods of microbial electrosynthesis for CO2 conversion, with labels indicating biofilm formation, electron flow, hydrogen production, and bio-products, emphasizing current CO2 conversion rates.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent studies show microbial electrosynthesis achieves CO₂ conversion efficiencies of around 55.4 g m−2 d−1, translating to significant potential for sustainable product synthesis.
Efficiency is influenced by reactor design, microbial strains, current density, feed composition, and operational parameters, all crucial for optimizing CO₂ conversion rates in microbial systems.

Microbial electrosynthesis offers advantages over traditional methods by utilizing renewable electricity and CO₂, resulting in lower carbon footprints and enhanced sustainability in chemical production.
